text: Zone 2 intensity markers show substantial variability with coefficients of variation ranging from 6% to 29% across different parameters, indicating that different methods of defining Z2 can yield meaningfully different training prescriptions.
Ventilatory Threshold 1 (VT1) and maximal fat oxidation (FatMax) show strong alignment as Zone 2 intensity markers, making them interchangeable anchors for low-intensity training prescription.
Fixed percentages of HRmax used to define Zone 2 exhibit wide individual differences and may inaccurately reflect metabolic responses, potentially leading to training at incorrect intensities.
Blood lactate thresholds used to define Zone 2 boundaries exhibit wide individual differences similar to fixed HR percentage methods, limiting their reliability as standardized markers.

Personalized Zone 2 prescriptions based on physiological measurements such as VT1 and FatMax provide more accurate alignment of training intensity with metabolic demands than standardized percentage-based methods.

Individual variability in physiological profiles necessitates individualized low-intensity training prescriptions to optimize endurance adaptations, rather than relying on population-based zone calculations.
